title: Data Types in Rust
content:
### Data Types in Rust

Rust is statically typed and every value has a known type at compile time. Types are divided into two categories: scalar and compound.

---

### Scalar Types

| Type | Category | Examples |
|------|----------|----------|
| Integers | Signed/Unsigned | `i32`, `u64`, `i8`, `usize` |
| Floats | Floating point | `f32`, `f64` |
| bool | Boolean | `true`, `false` |
| char | Unicode character | `'a'`, `'♥'`, `'🦀'` |

---

### Compound Types

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| Tuple | Group of values (possibly different types) | `(i32, f64, char)` |
| Array | Fixed-length, same type | `[i32; 5]` |
| Slice | Dynamic view into a sequence | `&[i32]` |
| String | Growable UTF-8 string | `String` |

```rust
let tup: (i32, f64, u8) = (500, 6.4, 1);
let arr: [i32; 3] = [1, 2, 3];
```

> **Key Point:** Rust uses type inference, so you often don't need to annotate types explicitly. However, you can always add type annotations for clarity or when inference is ambiguous.

quiz.question: Which type represents a 32-bit signed integer in Rust?
